summary: This case study details a practical approach to creating repeatable website and vendor flows using JSON sequences and a Chrome extension. The methodology leverages accessibility tree control and escalates to a DOM-grounded discovery or visible-text diff when necessary. The article provides actionable insights into managing complex automation tasks.
strengths: Provides a clear, practical methodology for automating website and vendor flows.
Addresses real-world challenges like UI churn and state management (TLS interstitials, consent banners).
Offers actionable advice on when to use different automation techniques (AX vs. extension path).
Documents a structured approach to managing automation scripts as versioned JSON files.
weaknesses: Lacks a supporting repository, making direct verification of the described flows impossible.
The novelty is limited as it builds upon existing concepts of UI automation and scripting.
Some technical details, like the 'localhost bridge' and 'vcc chrome-diff', are not fully elaborated.
suggestions: Provide a link to a public repository with example JSON flows and the associated automation tools.
Elaborate on the 'localhost bridge' and 'vcc chrome-diff' functionalities with more detail or examples.
Include a more concrete example of a site-specific flow, detailing the steps and the rationale for choosing a particular automation method.

claims.text: We maintain JSON sequence drafts for high-value paths such as analytics admin, tag manager, DNS, and email vendor consoles.
claims.source: General knowledge of common web application functionalities. No specific search needed as this describes a practice, not a verifiable fact.

claims.text: We default to accessibility-tree control for Safari and Chrome.
claims.source: General knowledge of browser automation capabilities. Both Safari and Chrome support accessibility tree inspection.

claims.text: We escalate to the MV3 Chrome extension and localhost bridge when the page needs DOM-grounded discovery or visible-text diff that AX alone does not expose cleanly.
claims.source: Chrome extensions (MV3) can interact with DOM. The concept of a localhost bridge for communication is a common pattern in automation.

novelty_vs_existing.title: "Browser Control Layer: Programmatic Desktop Automation via macOS Accessibility API for AI Agent Swarms"
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission focuses on a cross-browser (Safari/Chrome) and cross-platform (implied by extension use) approach using JSON sequences, whereas the existing paper is specific to macOS Accessibility API. This submission also details the use of a Chrome extension and DOM-grounded discovery, which is a different technical approach.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Singleton Control Planes and Mutex Discipline in Agent Swarms Using Shared Human Interfaces
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission is more about the practical implementation of automation flows for specific websites, while the existing paper discusses higher-level coordination and control plane concepts in agent swarms. The overlap is minimal, focusing on the 'shared human interfaces' aspect indirectly.

novelty_vs_existing.title: "Background macOS Desktop Automation via Accessibility API: Patterns for AI Agents in Multi-Agent Environments"
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: Similar to 2026-026, this paper is macOS-centric. This submission's contribution lies in its cross-browser strategy and the use of JSON sequences for defining flows, which is a more concrete and structured approach to defining automation tasks compared to general patterns.

supports.text: The concept of a localhost bridge for communication is a common pattern in automation.
